item.title: State board: Musk may have broken election law by offering $1M checks to voters
item.description: The Wisconsin Elections Commission found billionaire Elon Musk may have violated the state's election bribery law by offering $1 million checks to voters during an election last year.

item.title: CDC looking into several items potentially linked to cyclospora parasite
item.description: As of Tuesday, nearly 7,000 people in 34 states reported symptoms tied to a parasitic illness that, so far, has no official source. Carter Evans reports on what's suspected of being behind the cyclosporiasis outbreak.

item.title: Cop who rescued woman in crisis on Brooklyn Bridge was on his 3rd day with unit
item.description: Warning: sensitive content. Footage released Tuesday shows police officers successfully rescuing a woman who was in crisis on the Brooklyn Bridge. Officer Christian Yepes, who helped talk the woman off the bridge in what was just his third day with his unit, spoke with CBS News' Anna Schecter about the operation.

item.title: Trump reverses course on U.S. acting as "guardian of the Hormuz Strait"
item.description: The U.S. hit Iran with heavy airstrikes for the fourth night in a row, aimed at preventing the regime's new attacks on oil and cargo ships. After President Trump floated the idea of the U.S. acting as a "guardian of the Hormuz Strait" on Monday, he reversed his decision. Ed O'Keefe has the details.

item.title: Why so many fatal ICE shootings have happened in Trump's second term
item.description: During President Trump's second term, at least 11 people have been shot and killed by federal immigration officers, and many others have been caught up in high-profile public incidents. Camilo Montoya-Galvez looks into why there seems to be many such cases.

item.title: Federal officials suspend most vehicle stops during ICE operations after deadly shootings
item.description: After U.S. federal agents killed two men in less than a week, Immigration and Customs Enforcement told its officers to stop pulling over vehicles in most cases. Lilia Luciano reports.

item.title: U.S. blockade on Iran restarts as Trump angles for control over Strait of Hormuz
item.description: As the U.S. restarts its Iran blockade and carries out more strikes, President Trump has called off a planned 20% fee on shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Aide to Rep. Ro Khanna on moment he says he was detained by Israeli settlers in West Bank
item.description: Democratic Rep. Ro Khanna says he was detained by Israeli settlers during a visit to the West Bank last week. Cameron Kasky, an aide to Rep. Ro Khanna who was along on the trip, joined "The Takeout" to discuss.

item.title: A timeline of the Karen Read case
item.description: Here's a look at how events have unfolded in the Karen Read case.

item.title: 12 states sue to block Paramount-Warner Bros. Discovery merger
item.description: The suit poses a new challenge to the $110 billion deal that would unite two of the nation's largest media companies.

item.title: N.Y. enacts nation's first statewide moratorium on building new data centers
item.description: Building new data centers in New York will be paused for a year in order to allow state officials to establish guidelines protecting residents and the environment, Gov. Kathy Hochul said.

item.title: Key points from Supreme Court justices' push for more security funds
item.description: Supreme Court Justices Elena Kagan and Amy Coney Barrett on Tuesday urged Congress to boost security funding for the high court as the judges face increased threats. CBS News' Jan Crawford reports.

item.title: Rep. Michael McCaul on Lindsey Graham and Ukraine
item.description: Lindsey Graham's death leaves Ukraine without a key Republican ally in the Senate. Rep. Michael McCaul, who recently visited Ukraine and had worked extensively with Sen. Graham, joins "The Takeout" to discuss.

item.title: What to know about the Iran war as Trump changes course on 20% fee for Strait of Hormuz cargo
item.description: President Trump on Tuesday reversed his Monday proclamation about a 20% fee for cargo moving through the Strait of Hormuz. CBS News' Ed O'Keefe and Aaron MacLean report.

item.title: Taco Bell taking precautionary measures as cyclosporiasis outbreak spreads in Michigan
item.description: Michigan's outbreak of cyclosporiasis, a disease marked by severe diarrhea, intensified Tuesday, with 3,300 cases identified and 44 people hospitalized. On Tuesday, Taco Bell told CBS News that it has "voluntarily and temporarily removed limited ingredients at select restaurants as a precautionary measure." CBS News medical correspondent Dr. Celine Gounder reports on what might be causing the spread.

item.title: Feds turn over evidence on ICE shootings sought by Minnesota officials
item.description: Federal investigators have turned over evidence that officials in Minnesota have been requesting for months about the deadly ICE shootings the state experienced in January. Hennepin County attorney Mary Moriarty joins "The Takeout" to discuss.

item.title: Latest on ICE scrutiny after fatal shootings in Texas, Maine
item.description: Nine people in the U.S. have been killed by immigration officers in the last 18 months, including two in the last week in Texas and Maine. CBS News' Lilia Luciano and Ed O'Keefe report, while Hennepin County attorney Mary Moriarty joins "The Takeout" to discuss the investigation into two ICE killings in Minnesota.

item.title: ICE halts most vehicle stops following deadly shootings, sources say
item.description: Neither of the victims of the ICE shootings in Maine or Texas were the target of enforcement operations, according to the Department of Homeland Security.

item.title: House passes bill to make daylight saving time permanent
item.description: The measure also allows states to opt out if they take action before the federal law is enacted.

item.title: Spain reaches World Cup final with dominant 2-0 win over France
item.description: In a match Tuesday featuring two of soccer's biggest heavyweights, Spain put in a masterful performance, frustrating France to the tune of a 2-0 win to advance to Sunday's World Cup final​.

item.title: Cyclospora outbreak leaves consumers guessing which foods are safe
item.description: Officials are still searching for the source of the outbreak, prompting consumers to seek advice on social media about which foods to avoid.

item.title: Bipartisan senators introduce Russia sanctions and tariff bill Graham championed
item.description: The text of the bill had been finalized before Graham met with Zelenskyy last week in Kyiv.

item.title: U.S. marine biologist shot dead in the Philippines, companion injured
item.description: Kent Carpenter and his Filipina companion were at his house in the coastal town of Sibulan when masked men forced their way in.

item.title: AI companies are creating "generative ghosts" of deceased loved ones
item.description: Startups are using emails, photos and voice recordings to create AI simulations that family and friends can interact with after a loved one's death.

item.title: Family says they're forced to sell home to help power data centers
item.description: Georgia Power says building a new transmission line will require acquiring more than 300 parcels of land, including residential properties.

item.title: Starter Homes are a Million Bucks Now | Money Moves with Jill Schlesinger
item.description:
Starter homes now cost $1 million in many parts of the country, leaving younger generations wondering whether homeownership is still a realistic goal. Jill asks whether buying a house should remain a cornerstone of the American dream, or if the idea itself has become outdated. Plus, caller questions: Dorian is feeling the financial strain of wedding season as travel costs, gifts and pricey trips pile up. And Andrew, a new dad, wants to know whether now is the right time to buy term life insurance.
Have a money question? Email askjill@jillonmoney.com.

item.title: Don't Buy a House Together Until You Hear This | Money Moves with Jill Schlesinger
item.description:
Caller Aaron has a family mess on his hands: his cousin bought a house with a girlfriend; they broke up, and now his cousin's credit is still tied to the property while the ex continues living there and refuses to refinance. Jill explains exactly what to do before buying a major asset with someone you're not married to. Plus, Jill breaks down the new student loan rules that took effect on July 1, 2026, how parents and prospective students should navigate the new rules and why taking on debt for college may not be worth it anymore.
Have a money question? Email askjill@jillonmoney.com.

item.title: Boomer Parents: It's Time to Cough it Up | Money Moves with Jill Schlesinger
item.description:
Americans have been anxiously awaiting the so-called "great wealth transfer," where trillions of dollars are expected to pass down from Baby Boomers to their kids. But people are living longer and holding onto their assets. Jill says it's time to start opening up the purse strings now. Plus, caller questions: Toby asks whether sterling silver jewelry is actually a worthwhile investment, and Dave wants to know if taking a pay cut to improve his quality of life is financially realistic.
Have a money question? Email askjill@jillonmoney.com.

item.title: Capt. "Sully" returns to Hudson River, shares "biggest frustrations"
item.description: Captain "Sully" Sullenberger became a national hero after his life-saving splashdown on the Hudson, but wants people to know what happened after that, now the center of the new movie, "Sully"

item.title: Pilot Pulls Off "A Miracle On The Hudson"
item.description: Passengers, Officials Praise U.S. Airways Pilot For River Crash Landing
